description: Athletes with visual impairment — total or near-total blindness. Compete with a guide runner.
Athletes with visual impairment — partial sight with visual acuity up to 6/60.
Athletes with visual impairment — mildest form with visual acuity above 6/60.
Athletes with intellectual impairment affecting athletic performance.
Wheelchair athletes with coordination impairments affecting all four limbs — moderate trunk control.
Wheelchair athletes with coordination impairments affecting all four limbs — good trunk control.
Athletes with coordination impairments — can run with some difficulty. Affects all four limbs.
Athletes with coordination impairments — moderate walking ability affecting all four limbs.
Athletes with coordination impairments — walk and run with asymmetric gait. Affects one side.
Athletes with coordination impairments — mild coordination issues. Close to full running ability.
Athletes of short stature — standing height under 130cm for men or 125cm for women.
Athletes of short stature — standing height 130-145cm for men or 125-137cm for women.
Athletes with single above-knee amputation or equivalent limb deficiency. Compete without prosthesis.
Athletes with double below-knee amputation or equivalent. May use running prostheses.
Athletes with below-knee amputation or limb impairment affecting one or both legs. Running prostheses common.
Athletes with upper limb deficiency — amputation or limb difference affecting one or both arms.
Athletes with unilateral upper limb deficiency — single arm affected below the elbow.
Wheelchair racers — limited shoulder and arm function. No trunk or leg function.
Wheelchair racers — functional arms but no trunk or leg function.
Wheelchair racers — full arm function with some trunk control. No leg function.
Wheelchair racers — full arm and partial trunk function. Some leg function possible.
Athletes with below-knee amputation using running prostheses — bilateral (both legs).
Athletes with above-knee amputation using running prostheses — bilateral.
Athletes with above-knee amputation using a running prosthesis — unilateral (one leg).
Athletes with below-knee amputation using running prostheses — unilateral.
Swimmers with significant physical impairment in all limbs — most limited functional ability.
Swimmers with severe limb impairments but slightly more function than S1.
Swimmers with moderate limb impairments or significant trunk involvement.
Swimmers with use of arms but no trunk/leg function or significant four-limb impairments.
Swimmers with full arm function but limited trunk/leg ability.
Swimmers with moderate physical impairments — some trunk control and limited leg function.
Swimmers with one side affected or moderate coordination impairment.
Swimmers with mild physical impairment — limb deficiency or mild coordination issues.
Swimmers with minimal physical impairment — single below-knee amputation or equivalent.
Swimmers with very mild impairment — minimal impact on swimming performance.
Swimmers with total or near-total visual impairment. Wear blackout goggles.
Swimmers with partial visual impairment — moderate sight loss.
Swimmers with mild visual impairment — least severe visual classification.
Swimmers with intellectual impairment affecting swimming performance.
Players with most significant functional limitations — minimal trunk movement.
Players with moderate trunk control — can lean forward and to sides.
Athletes with cerebral palsy — can throw the ball. May use hands or feet.
Athletes with cerebral palsy — better arm function than BC1. Throw with hand.
Athletes with severe impairment in all four limbs — use a ramp to propel the ball. Assistant required.
Athletes with severe physical impairment (non-cerebral palsy) — use a ramp.
Shooters who can support the weight of the firearm independently. Pistol and rifle.
Shooters who cannot support firearm weight — use a stand. Rifle only.
